name: Bexar County, Texas
tagline: The 4th-most-populous Texas county (~2M residents, San Antonio) runs its technology through Bexar County Information Technology (BCIT) on a ~$2.8B county budget — and sits inside San Antonio's deep cyber ecosystem, now anchored by the state's new Texas Cyber Command.
overview: Bexar County is the fourth-most-populous county in Texas, home to roughly 2 million residents and the City of San Antonio. Its central technology organization is Bexar County Information Technology (BCIT), the internal IT provider for county offices and departments — from the Auditor to the Sheriff — delivering computing and communications infrastructure, end-user services, enterprise applications, and cybersecurity on a 24/7 basis. Governance runs through a five-member Commissioners Court (the County Judge plus four precinct Commissioners), which adopts the budget and approves contracts of size; day-to-day administration runs through the County Manager, to whom the CIO reports. For a Dell SLG seller, the practical map is: BCIT is the technology buying center, the County Manager and Budget & Finance shape what gets funded, and Commissioners Court is the approval gate. Bexar is also distinctive for its setting — San Antonio holds one of the largest concentrations of cyber talent in the U.S. (Sixteenth Air Force, NSA, UTSA, and federal partners), and the new state-level Texas Cyber Command is standing up locally — which raises both the security expectations and the partnership options around the county.

budget.intro: Bexar County Commissioners Court unanimously adopted a ~$2.8B FY2026 budget (fiscal year Oct 1, 2025–Sep 30, 2026) without raising the property-tax rate, holding it at $0.299999 per $100. Technology is a focused line within that: the proposed FY25-26 budget carried $150M+ in technology-related spending, with BCIT itself slated for a ~$45.6M operating budget — a ~$6.2M increase year-over-year for infrastructure, cybersecurity, end-user services, and enterprise applications.

stats.label: FY2026 county budget
stats.note: Adopted Sep 9, 2025; flat to prior year, no tax-rate increase

stats.label: BCIT budget
stats.note: FY25-26; a ~$6.2M YoY increase for central IT

stats.label: Total tech-related spend
stats.note: Proposed FY25-26 across IT ops, infra, public safety, digital equity

notes: Officials have flagged a looming multi-year shortfall (reports cite a ~$145M future deficit) while resisting a tax-rate increase — expect cost-optimization and consolidation to stay front of mind.

Tech funding also includes a ~$6.5M Technology Improvement Fund for equipment and ~$5.9M for the BiblioTech digital-library system; the largest IT-adjacent spends still sit with operating offices (Sheriff, courts).
BCIT is the central path, not the only wallet — confirm which office owns a given program before forecasting.

initiatives.name: Bexar 2.0 modernization
initiatives.summary: A ~$27.3M initiative to improve county employee and resident remote-work capability through IT-infrastructure investment, automation, and cloud-based document storage — the county's largest named technology project and a clear window for client refresh, infrastructure, and as-a-service.

initiatives.name: Core infrastructure refresh
initiatives.summary: BCIT is upgrading its Cisco HyperFlex compute/storage platform (vendor support ended September 2025), which underpins most county applications not hosted in the cloud. A direct ISG and data-center modernization opportunity tied to an end-of-support forcing function.

initiatives.name: IoT / network security
initiatives.summary: A BCIT IoT Network Security project secures roughly 14,000 daily Internet-of-Things devices on the county network — a recurring security and endpoint-visibility opportunity, reinforced by a modernized data center adopting a cloud-first, IP-monitoring security posture.

initiatives.name: AI governance (Admin Policy 5.3)
initiatives.summary: Commissioners Court unanimously adopted Administrative Policy 5.3 to govern AI use across departments, directing BCIT to vet vendors, run an employee-awareness campaign, and coordinate with partners handling sensitive data — an early governance gate that shapes how AI tooling can be sold in.

procurement.intro: Bexar County buys through Bexar County Purchasing, with awards of size approved by Commissioners Court. As a local government it is also eligible to buy off pre-competed cooperative vehicles — most importantly the Texas DIR Cooperative Contracts — which let it acquire technology at state pricing without running a full county RFP. Confirm Dell's coverage on the right vehicles before pursuing a deal.

vehicles.name: Bexar County Purchasing + Commissioners Court
vehicles.detail: Purchasing runs solicitations; Commissioners Court approval is the gate for significant awards. As of Jan 2026, the county delegated contract-approval authority up to $100K to department heads (raised from $50K) to speed procurement — awards above $100K still require a court vote. Texas interested-parties disclosure applies to court-approved contracts.

vehicles.name: Texas DIR Cooperative Contracts
vehicles.detail: As a local government, Bexar County can buy technology off DIR's pre-competed co-op at State of Texas pricing — the fastest compliant path that avoids a full county RFP. Verify Dell's DIR contract scope covers the products in play.

vehicles.name: Other purchasing cooperatives
vehicles.detail: The county also leverages national/interlocal cooperatives for goods and services — confirm which vehicle a given technology category is bought through.

powerStructure.name: Peter Sakai
powerStructure.title: Bexar County Judge
powerStructure.note: Top elected executive and Commissioners Court chair; lost the March 2026 Democratic primary to Ron Nirenberg and serves through December 2026. In the Nov 2026 general election Nirenberg (D) faces Patrick Von Dohlen (R) — a leadership transition is coming; build durable relationships below the top office.

powerStructure.name: David L. Smith
powerStructure.title: County Manager
powerStructure.note: Runs day-to-day county administration and budget; the CIO reports to him. A key relationship for funding and cross-department technology programs.

powerStructure.name: Mark Gager
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer, BCIT
powerStructure.note: Leads central IT and reports to the County Manager; PMP- and Balanced-Scorecard-certified, ex-CIO/Chief Strategy Officer at BioBridge Global. The top technology relationship for the account.

powerStructure.name: Commissioners Court
powerStructure.title: County Judge + 4 precinct Commissioners
powerStructure.note: Adopts the budget and approves contracts of size — the decision gate for any major deal.

powerStructure.name: BCIT Information Security function
powerStructure.title: Cybersecurity / network security
powerStructure.note: Owns the county's security posture (IoT security, data-center monitoring). Confirm the named security lead; note adjacency to San Antonio's cyber ecosystem and the new Texas Cyber Command at UTSA.

news.title: FY2027 budget crunch: county to cut, not raise taxes, amid worst revenue year since 2008
news.impact: County Manager David Smith projects property-tax revenue down ~1.5-1.7% in FY2026-27 (about 80% of the general fund) and plans to close the gap with spending cuts rather than a rate increase, holding the current $0.276331 rate; a ~$145M multi-year deficit still looms toward FY2028-29. Budget town halls ran June 24-25 with a formal proposal due this fall. Lead hard with cost-optimization, consolidation, standardization, and TCO — new-money asks will face scrutiny.

news.title: Commissioners Court delegates contract authority up to $100K to department heads
news.impact: Raised the staff-approval threshold from $50K to $100K (enabled by the latest Texas legislative session) to speed procurement; awards above $100K still go to Commissioners Court. Smaller technology buys can now move without a court vote — map which BCIT purchases now clear at the staff level versus the court gate.

news.title: Texas Cyber Command discloses breach affecting 3M+ license customers
news.impact: The new San Antonio-based, UTSA-hosted Cyber Command is now operational and active in incident response (TPWD hunting/fishing license vendor breach) — raises regional security expectations and signals partnership/standards a Dell security motion should align to.

news.title: Sakai's final State of the County: growth focus amid fiscal pressure
news.impact: In his final address (May 27), outgoing Judge Sakai framed Bexar as a 'regional powerhouse' while officials flagged food security, housing, workforce, and economic development as priorities under a projected ~2% property-tax decline / ~$145M deficit — a cost-conscious backdrop for FY27 tech planning.

news.title: County Judge Sakai loses Democratic primary; Nirenberg to face Republican Von Dohlen in November
news.impact: Sakai (~37.8%) lost to former San Antonio Mayor Ron Nirenberg (~62.2%) and serves through Dec 2026; Nirenberg faces Republican Patrick Von Dohlen in the Nov 2026 general election. Anchor relationships at the BCIT and County Manager layer that survive the top-of-county transition.

news.title: Commissioners Court adopts ~$2.8B FY2026 budget (no tax-rate hike)
news.impact: Flat budget with a flagged future shortfall sets a cost-conscious FY26 envelope, including a ~$45.6M BCIT line — lead with cost-optimization, consolidation, and standardization value.

dellActions: Map the buying center precisely: BCIT (CIO Mark Gager) as the technology owner, the County Manager (David L. Smith) and Budget & Finance for funding, and Commissioners Court as the approval gate — sequence the deal accordingly.
Confirm Dell's Texas DIR Cooperative Contract and other co-op coverage so the county can buy at state pricing without a full RFP — the fastest compliant path.
Lead with the core-infrastructure (Cisco HyperFlex end-of-support) refresh and Bexar 2.0 modernization for ISG, client, and cloud-adjacent positioning tied to stated FY26 priorities.
Bring a cost-optimization, standardization, and consolidation narrative — the budget is flat with a flagged future shortfall, so value and TCO will carry the conversation.
Engage early on AI governance (Admin Policy 5.3) and the San Antonio cyber context (Texas Cyber Command at UTSA) to position secure, compliant AI-infrastructure and endpoint/security offerings.
